title: Before Apps, There Were Green Stamps: The History of Customer Loyalty
summary: Before there were smartphone apps and digital points, there were Green Stamps. The idea of rewarding loyal customers is ancient — Egyptian merchants gave tokens for bread, and a New England merchant in the 1700s offered copper tokens. But the first large-scale loyalty program began in 1896, when traveling salesman Thomas Sperry and investor Shelley Byron Hutchinson founded the Sperry & Hutchinson Company (S&H). They issued small green-and-white paper stamps at stores in Jackson, Michigan. For every 10 cents spent, customers received a stamp worth 10 points. They would lick the stamp and paste it into a booklet. When a booklet was full, they could redeem it for merchandise at S&H showrooms. One booklet could get you a porcelain lamp; six booklets could get you a bicycle. The program exploded. By the early 1900s, S&H launched a catalog that became one of the most widely circulated publications in the US. By 1964, S&H was distributing 32 million catalogs a year, and customers redeemed more than a billion stamps weekly. At its peak, there were 800 S&H redemption centers and over 100,000 participating businesses. The stamps even appeared in pop culture — Andy Warhol made a 1962 silkscreen of them, now at the Museum of Modern Art. But the good times didn't last. In the mid-1970s, the US economy stagnated, and S&H began to decline. By the late 1980s, fewer than 100 retailers still participated. Many stores decided to cut out the middleman and offer their own rewards programs. S&H tried to go digital in 2000, rebranding as "Greenpoints," but it never recaptured the magic. Today, loyalty programs are bigger than ever — they accounted for $12 billion in economic activity in 2024. Yet consumers leave a huge amount on the table: globally, only 18% of loyalty points are used. In the US, 26.2% of points go unspent, worth up to $10 billion annually. So next time you earn points, remember the Green Stamps — and don't forget to redeem them!
why_it_matters: The story of Green Stamps explains where today's loyalty programs come from and shows that even though these programs are worth billions, most people leave money on the table—a lesson in being a smart consumer.

keywords.explanation: Small green-and-white paper stamps given to customers as rewards for shopping, which could be collected and exchanged for gifts.
A system where businesses reward customers for repeated purchases, often with points or stamps.
The company that created and ran the Green Stamps program, often called S&H.
A book filled with pictures and descriptions of items that people could order using their collected stamps.
Physical stores where customers could exchange their filled stamp booklets for merchandise.
The unsuccessful digital update of the Green Stamps program launched in 2000.

questions.question: What could customers get for six full booklets of Green Stamps?
How many stamps were customers redeeming weekly at the peak?
What caused the decline of Green Stamps in the mid-1970s?
What percentage of loyalty points are used globally, according to the article?

background_read: Thomas Sperry (a traveling salesman) and Shelley Byron Hutchinson founded the Sperry & Hutchinson Company (S&H, the company that created Green Stamps) in 1896 in Jackson, Michigan.
At its peak in the 1960s, S&H had 800 redemption centers and over 100,000 businesses giving out stamps, and its catalog was one of the most widely distributed publications in the US.
The program declined in the 1970s as the economy slowed and stores began their own loyalty programs, cutting out the middleman.
S&H tried to go digital in 2000 with 'Greenpoints', but it never became popular. Today, loyalty programs are huge, but globally only 18% of earned points are actually used.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Contrast between today's digital points and old paper Green Stamps
└─ Hook: 'Before there were smartphone apps and digital points, there were Green Stamps.'
HISTORICAL CONTEXT: Ancient tokens and 1700s copper tokens as early loyalty rewards
└─ FIRST LARGE-SCALE PROGRAM: S&H Green Stamps founded 1896
├─ For every 10 cents spent, get a stamp worth 10 points
└─ Redeem at showrooms: e.g., 1 booklet = porcelain lamp, 6 = bicycle
PEAK: By 1964, 32 million catalogs distributed, 1 billion stamps redeemed weekly, 800 centers, 100,000 businesses
POP CULTURE: Andy Warhol silkscreen (1962) at Museum of Modern Art
DECLINE: Mid-1970s economy stagnated, stores started own programs; by late 1980s fewer than 100 retailers
DIGITAL ATTEMPT: Greenpoints in 2000, didn't succeed
MODERN STATS: $12 billion economic activity, only 18% of points used globally; US $10 billion unspent
CLOSING: Call to action—remember Green Stamps and redeem your points

perspectives.description: Customers benefited from free merchandise without spending extra money, and businesses gained loyal repeat customers. The system created a sense of reward and community around saving stamps.
The program encouraged impulse spending to collect stamps, and the stamps themselves were wasteful paper. The decline also hurt employees and communities that depended on redemption centers.

Green Stamps represent an early successful example of a third-party loyalty scheme. Its rise and fall illustrate how economic conditions and competition shape business models. The modern digital versions are more efficient but still leave huge amounts of points unused.

As loyalty programs become more integrated with apps and data, the risk of customers forgetting or failing to redeem points persists. Companies may need to simplify redemption or face regulation. The lesson of Green Stamps is that even a popular program can disappear if it doesn't adapt.
